title: Reading and Use of English - Part 2
description: For questions 9 - 16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: The invention of photography in the nineteenth century transformed how humanity documented the world, (0) which created an entirely new relationship between image and reality. Few innovations have so radically altered our perception of time and memory. (9) Although early photographic techniques required subjects to remain motionless for extended periods, the possibilities they unlocked were extraordinary. (10) Such was the public appetite for the daguerreotype, introduced in 1839, that portrait studios spread rapidly across European and American cities. Customers who could never have afforded a painted likeness were now able to own a faithful image of themselves, and (11) Provided that they could access a studio, even families of modest means participated in this new visual culture. Not (12) only did photography transform how individuals recorded their lives, but it also fundamentally altered the status and purpose of portrait painting. (13) Regardless of the ongoing debates among artists and critics about whether photography constituted art, its role in documentation was irreversible. Not (14) until the development of the dry-plate process in the 1870s did photography become genuinely accessible to the general public. (15) While many established painters viewed the new medium with suspicion, some quickly adapted their practice in response to its challenge. The dialogue between painting and photography has shaped visual art ever since. (16) Whether photography is best understood as a scientific instrument or a creative medium, its contribution to human culture has been immeasurable.

example.text: The invention of photography in the nineteenth century transformed how humanity documented the world, (0) which created an entirely new relationship between image and reality.

questions.explanation: Concessive conjunction used to introduce a contrast or concession between two clauses.
Emphatic determiner in the inverted structure 'Such was/is the [noun] that...' to express a high degree.
Conditional conjunction meaning 'on the condition that', used to introduce a condition and its result.
Part of the correlative conjunction 'Not only... but also', which requires subject-auxiliary inversion after 'Not only'.
Part of the fixed prepositional phrase 'Regardless of', meaning 'without being affected by'.
Part of the 'Not until' negative fronting structure, which requires subject-auxiliary inversion.
Concessive conjunction meaning 'even though' or 'despite the fact that', introducing a contrast between two different reactions.
Conjunction introducing two alternatives or possibilities in a summarising conclusion.
